title: Class interface and invariants

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C++: Beginner to Intermediate draft for Class interface and invariants; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: C++ Core Guidelines; living guidelines accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Class interface and invariants
sources.claim: A class protects invariants by exposing operations that keep state valid and hiding representation details that callers should not coordinate directly.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A class protects invariants by exposing operations that keep state valid and hiding representation details that callers should not coordinate directly.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A BankAccount keeps balance private and allows deposit only for positive amounts, preserving its documented invariant. Your goal is to design a class whose constructor and methods maintain stated invariants. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Value, reference and const-reference parameters; pf data records; Function contracts and return values. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: A class protects invariants by exposing operations that keep state valid and hiding representation details that callers should not coordinate directly. This rule is applied to concrete C++ source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Access control is compile-time, friends and references can expose state, getters can leak mutable internals, and serialization may couple to representation. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: Encapsulation means adding getters and setters for every private field, regardless of invariants.

includes: Hide representation and expose operations that preserve valid state.
Design a class whose constructor and methods maintain stated invariants.
excludes: Access control is compile-time, friends and references can expose state, getters can leak mutable internals, and serialization may couple to representation.

outcomes: Design a class whose constructor and methods maintain stated invariants.

examples.title: Class interface and invariants: defined case
examples.situation: A BankAccount keeps balance private and allows deposit only for positive amounts, preserving its documented invariant.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: A BankAccount keeps balance private and allows deposit only for positive amounts, preserving its documented invariant.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: Expose domain operations, not automatic field access; each public operation should preserve the class invariant and hide replaceable representation.
examples.result: The defined result follows because Expose domain operations, not automatic field access; each public operation should preserve the class invariant and hide replaceable representation.

examples.title: Class interface and invariants: boundary case
examples.situation: A collection returns a read-only view instead of a mutable reference that could bypass validation.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: A collection returns a read-only view instead of a mutable reference that could bypass validation.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Access control is compile-time, friends and references can expose state, getters can leak mutable internals, and serialization may couple to representation.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: Encapsulation means adding getters and setters for every private field, regardless of invariants.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Design a class whose constructor and methods maintain stated invariants.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Encapsulation means adding getters and setters for every private field, regardless of invariants.
misconceptions.correction: Expose domain operations, not automatic field access; each public operation should preserve the class invariant and hide replaceable representation.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete CPP rules, explain why this belief fails: Encapsulation means adding getters and setters for every private field, regardless of invariants.

analogy.example: A system of self-managing components can model Class interface and invariants: each component protects an invariant and owns resources through typed interfaces and lifetime.
usefulFor: Connecting types, value semantics, invariants, generic interfaces, ownership, and deterministic cleanup
limitations: C++ overload resolution, templates, value categories, object lifetime, and undefined behavior have no exact physical equivalent.
Access control is compile-time, friends and references can expose state, getters can leak mutable internals, and serialization may couple to representation.
